title: Thermodynamic phase transitions reveal the resilience structure of urban traffic congestion
abstract: Understanding how cities transition from free-flowing to congested traffic remains a central open problem in urban science. Here we show that city-scale congestion undergoes a reproducible nonlinear transition analogous to an order-disorder phase transition in statistical mechanics, in which aggregate mobility acts as a control parameter and jam extent as a collective order parameter. Crucially, this analogy is not merely formal: we derive and empirically identify an effective thermodynamic temperature with concrete physical meaning, quantifying infrastructural heterogeneity and how broadly a city explores congestion configurations as demand increases. Low-temperature cities are congestion-fragile: small mobility increases trigger sharp, system-wide jam transitions. This framework further reveals that the macroscopic fundamental diagram is an incomplete description of the traffic state: it emerges as a projection of a richer free-energy landscape governed by entropy-capacity trade-offs. Validated across 46 cities in Latin America and the Caribbean and independently confirmed with loop-detector data from 8 cities on three continents, these results establish a physics-based foundation for comparing urban traffic resilience and anticipating congestion regime shifts under changing mobility demand.
summary: This paper demonstrates that urban traffic congestion behaves like a thermodynamic phase transition. By defining an effective temperature for cities, the authors can predict which urban infrastructures are prone to sudden, system-wide traffic jams.
main_problem: Understanding the transition of urban traffic from free-flowing to congested states and identifying the underlying drivers of traffic resilience.
main_result: City-scale congestion is shown to undergo a nonlinear phase transition analogous to an order-disorder transition, where an effective thermodynamic temperature quantifies infrastructural heterogeneity and predicts congestion fragility.
method: The study applies statistical mechanics frameworks to large-scale urban mobility data, deriving an effective thermodynamic temperature and analyzing a free-energy landscape.
model_or_system: The system is urban traffic networks across 46 cities in Latin America and the Caribbean, plus 8 additional cities globally, treated as a many-body system where aggregate mobility is the control parameter.
key_observables: Aggregate mobility (control parameter), jam extent (order parameter), and an effective thermodynamic temperature.
important_parameters: Aggregate mobility, infrastructural heterogeneity (via effective temperature), and entropy-capacity trade-offs.
main_assumptions: The paper assumes the traffic transition can be mapped onto a formal thermodynamic phase transition framework.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the phase transition analogy, derives the effective thermodynamic temperature, presents the free-energy landscape, and validates the framework using multi-city empirical data.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
